event: Scenes from the life and martyrdom of Saint James the Greater
symbols: architectural settings — the Roman world in which the early Church spread
baptism scene — sacramental initiation and conversion of pagans
trial before king — witness of faith under persecution
execution — apostolic martyrdom as supreme witness to Christ
description: A composite image showing multiple fresco panels from Mantegna's cycle in the Ovetari Chapel, depicting episodes from the life of St. James the Greater. The scenes include James baptizing the magician Hermogenes, James before Herod Agrippa, and James being led to execution. The frescoes employ dramatic perspective, classical architecture, and monumental figure compositions rendered in Mantegna's characteristic sharp, sculptural style.
theological_description: Mantegna's fresco cycle narrates the apostolic mission and martyrdom of James son of Zebedee, one of Christ's inner circle of disciples. According to Acts 12:1-2, Herod Agrippa I had James killed by the sword, making him the first apostle to be martyred. The baptism of Hermogenes, drawn from the Golden Legend, illustrates the triumph of apostolic preaching over pagan magic and the power of the Gospel to convert even its adversaries. The trial and execution scenes embody the theology of martyrdom central to early Christianity: that bearing witness unto death conforms the disciple to Christ's own Passion (Phil 1:21, Rev 2:10). The narrative arc from mission to martyrdom encapsulates the apostolic vocation as expressed in Christ's prophecy that James would indeed drink His cup (Mark 10.38-39).
